followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: Added SRG-induced many-body force truncation as a distinct technical objection (incorporating codex's valid point). Maintained 'promising' verdict against pull toward 'weak_evidence' from other reviewers. Adjusted reviewer confidence slightly upward (0.30 to 0.32) — minimal change because paywall limitation is unchanged. Retained all prior objections.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: Gemini assigns a 2/10 overall score and 'fatal' severity to the paywall limitation. This conflates 'we cannot review this paper' with 'this paper is wrong or misleading.' A PRL Editors' Suggestion that passed rigorous peer review should not receive a score implying it is 'likely wrong or not review-ready' (calibration: 0–2) simply because our bundle is incomplete. The correct response is to lower reviewer confidence, not the paper score.

Gemini's reviewer_confidence of 9/10 is internally inconsistent: claiming high confidence in an assessment made from abstract-only access is epistemically unjustified. A reviewer who cannot read the paper should have low confidence in their paper-level judgment.

Codex's framing of the paywall as a 'major' technical objection to the paper (rather than a limitation of the review bundle) is a category error. The paper's technical quality is not impugned by our access limitations; reviewer confidence should bear the cost of that limitation.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: Codex correctly emphasizes that SRG-induced many-body forces (3N, 4N) and many-body truncation errors are a real technical concern that the abstract does not address; I have added this as a distinct objection.
Codex's point that the quantitative diagnostic for spin/pseudospin symmetry is not defined in the abstract is valid — the 'transition' needs an operational measure to be meaningful.
Both codex and gemini are correct that abstract-only access severely limits review quality; this is a real constraint on all scores.

main_claims.claim: Magic numbers beyond 20 emerge naturally during a spin-to-pseudospin symmetry transition as momentum resolution scale decreases under SRG evolution of chiral EFT interactions.
main_claims.notes: Central claim; cannot audit calculations. Conceptual tension persists: standard magic numbers require spin-orbit splitting while pseudospin symmetry implies near-degeneracy suppressing it. The paper may resolve this via pseudo-orbital gap structure but this is not stated in the abstract.

main_claims.claim: The spin-to-pseudospin transition under SRG evolution is novel and previously unidentified.
main_claims.notes: Pseudospin symmetry in nuclei is extensively studied. The SRG-scale-evolution framing connecting it to magic number emergence may be genuinely new, but differentiation from Ginocchio, V_low-k universality literature, and relativistic mean-field work cannot be verified from the abstract.

main_claims.claim: Relativistic one-boson-exchange potentials yield a similar pattern, establishing robustness.
main_claims.notes: Meaningful cross-framework check in principle. Both interaction classes are constrained to reproduce low-energy NN data, so low-resolution SRG-evolved forms may converge by universality rather than providing a truly independent test.

main_claims.claim: This constitutes a first-principles explanation for the origin of magic numbers and pseudospin symmetry.
main_claims.notes: Chiral EFT with SRG is field-standard 'ab initio'. SRG is unitary, so physical observables are preserved; the 'explanation' may be a change of effective description rather than new physical content. Whether the paper distinguishes scale-dependent shell gaps from scale-independent observables is critical.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Shell gaps in a mean-field single-particle spectrum are a meaningful proxy for physical magic numbers across resolution scales.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: SRG evolution changes the effective mean-field basis. Apparent shell gaps may change without the underlying many-body spectrum changing. The claim requires clarification of whether 'emergence of magic numbers' refers to mean-field gaps or scale-independent spectroscopic observables.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The spin-to-pseudospin transition under SRG is sharp or well-defined rather than a gradual crossover.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: If this is a smooth interpolation, 'emergence' at a specific scale is less crisp, and the claimed connection to pseudospin symmetry as an organizing principle is weaker.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Independence of OBE and chiral EFT results.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Both are constrained to reproduce low-energy NN data. Low-resolution SRG-evolved forms may converge by universality (V_low-k universality), making agreement a consequence of shared infrared constraints rather than an independent robustness test.

missing_assumptions.assumption: SRG-induced many-body forces are either negligible or consistently included over the range of resolution scales examined.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: Truncation of induced 3N and higher-body operators can introduce spurious scale dependence in computed shell structure, potentially mimicking or masking the claimed transition.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the paper distinguish between scale-dependent effective single-particle shell gaps and scale-independent physical observables (binding energy differences, spectroscopic factors) when claiming magic numbers 'emerge'? If only mean-field gaps are tracked, the 'first-principles explanation' framing may be overstated.
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Full article text is paywalled; requires reading the methods and observables sections.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: How is the spin-to-pseudospin 'transition' defined operationally? Is there a quantitative symmetry metric whose evolution with SRG parameter λ is plotted, and is there a well-defined scale at which it crosses? Is this the same scale at which magic numbers appear?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Requires reading methodology and figures in the full paper.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Are SRG-induced many-body forces (3N and higher) consistently included or explicitly shown to be negligible over the range of resolution scales studied? If they are truncated, is there a sensitivity analysis?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This requires inspecting the computational methods section and supplemental material, both paywalled.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Does the paper explicitly differentiate from Ginocchio's relativistic explanation of pseudospin symmetry and from the universality-of-low-momentum-interactions program (V_low-k, Bogner et al.)? How does the claimed explanation go beyond known results?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Requires reading the introduction and reference list, both paywalled.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the OBE comparison genuinely independent evidence, or does the convergence follow from universality of low-momentum interactions (i.e., both frameworks collapse to similar V_low-k by construction)?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Requires reading the comparison methodology and understanding what scale mapping is used between frameworks.

novelty_assessment: The explicit connection between SRG-scale evolution and a spin-to-pseudospin symmetry transition as the mechanism for generating nuclear magic numbers appears to be a genuinely new organizing framework. Pseudospin symmetry itself is well-studied and its relativistic origin in nuclear potentials is established (Ginocchio et al.), and SRG evolution of nuclear forces is a mature technique (Bogner, Schwenk, et al.). The novel element is the claimed quantitative demonstration that traversing resolution scales drives a symmetry transition that produces magic number structure, offering a scale-bridging mechanistic account. PRL Editors' Suggestion status provides independent community endorsement of novelty and significance. The novelty is primarily conceptual and mechanistic: a new lens on known phenomena rather than prediction of new magic numbers. Full assessment of novelty differentiation from prior literature requires reading the introduction and references, which are paywalled.
score_rationale: Score is unchanged from cycle 1 at 5.5. The paper is a PRL Editors' Suggestion — strong independent evidence that PRL referees found the technical content sound and novel — which provides a floor that abstract-only reviewers cannot override without positive evidence of error. The SRG-induced many-body force concern (added from codex) is real but is a standard technical issue that experienced nuclear structure groups (including Bogner, a co-author) routinely address; it is unlikely to have been missed. The conceptual tension between pseudospin symmetry and magic number generation remains the key unresolved concern from the abstract, but may be resolved by a pseudo-orbital gap mechanism. Technical soundness is held at 6 (not higher) because I cannot audit the calculations. Reviewer confidence remains 3 reflecting paywall limitation and specialized subfield. Gemini's 2/10 score is rejected as methodologically unsound (conflating bundle incompleteness with paper failure). Codex's 4.2 is more defensible but still underweights the PRL Editors' Suggestion signal.
summary: This PRL Editors' Suggestion (February 2026) investigates how nuclear shell structure evolves with momentum resolution in SRG-evolved chiral EFT interactions, claiming a novel spin-to-pseudospin symmetry transition during which magic numbers emerge naturally, with independent confirmation from one-boson-exchange potentials. Review remains abstract-only due to a paywall. The paper is a published, peer-reviewed PRL Editors' Suggestion, which provides strong external evidence of technical quality independent of what reviewers can audit here. Conceptual tensions flagged in cycle 1 remain unresolved from the abstract, but are expected to be addressed in the full paper.

technical_objections.evidence: Standard nuclear physics: single-particle energies are scheme-dependent; SRG unitarity preserves S-matrix observables but not effective field operators. Claiming magic numbers 'emerge' under SRG may be demonstrating a change in effective description.
technical_objections.objection: SRG unitarity makes 'emergence' claims scheme-dependent. Shell gaps in a mean-field effective single-particle picture are not strict observables and vary with resolution scale by construction. The paper needs to show that the physical bound-state spectrum, not just the effective single-particle picture, supports the magic number claim.

technical_objections.evidence: Shell model phenomenology: magic numbers 28, 50, 82, 126 arise from large spin-orbit splitting. Pseudospin symmetry: pseudo-spin-orbit splittings are small. These are in tension unless a different mechanism (e.g., pseudo-orbital level ordering) generates the gaps.
technical_objections.objection: Conceptual tension between pseudospin symmetry and magic number generation. Mayer-Jensen magic numbers beyond 20 require spin-orbit splitting large enough to push intruder levels across shell gaps. Pseudospin symmetry implies near-degenerate doublets, generically reducing effective spin-orbit splitting. How decreasing spin-orbit character at low resolution simultaneously generates magic-number shell gaps is not explained in the abstract.

technical_objections.evidence: Standard technical concern in SRG applications: Bogner, Schwenk et al. have shown that many-body induced forces become important below certain resolution scales. The abstract does not address this.
technical_objections.objection: SRG-induced many-body forces may distort the shell-gap evolution. When SRG evolves an NN interaction to lower resolution, it generates induced 3N, 4N, ... operators. If higher-body induced terms are truncated inconsistently, the claimed resolution-scale dependence of shell structure may be a truncation artifact rather than a physical transition.
technical_objections.paper_location: Abstract: 'using the similarity renormalization group to link different scales'

technical_objections.evidence: Extensive published literature on pseudospin symmetry, relativistic origins, and low-momentum universality. The abstract does not differentiate the new claim from these.
technical_objections.objection: Novelty differentiation from prior pseudospin symmetry literature is not established in the abstract. The connection between pseudospin symmetry and relativistic nuclear potentials (Ginocchio 1997, 2005) and SRG universality of low-momentum interactions (V_low-k, Bogner et al.) are directly relevant prior art.

technical_objections.evidence: The periodic table in chemistry is determined by electron orbitals, not nuclear magic numbers. Nuclear magic numbers influence isotope stability but do not reorder the periodic table.
technical_objections.objection: Popular science framing ('could change the periodic table') does not match the paper's subject. The paper addresses nuclear magic numbers (proton/neutron shell closures), not atomic electron configuration or chemistry.
technical_objections.paper_location: Metadata: Sabine video title (not in paper itself)
